PRD Conversation - Whole 
PRD picks permissive; wants your confirmation.
  - A) Permissive (PRD default): always compute; display as equal peer to Vimshottari.
  - B) Strict BPHS 47.2: only when Moon in krishna paksha + Lagna has benefic
  occupation/aspect + night birth. Skip otherwise.
  - C) Simplified strict: just krishna paksha check; ignore the Lagna-benefic +
  night-birth clauses.
  - D) Permissive default but emit a classical_strictness: applicable|not_applicable flag
   on output so the UI can surface it.
  - E) Off by default; astrologer/user opts in per chart.

  ---
  Q3 — Yogini starting-nakshatra lookup table source.
  - A) BPHS Ch.48 table (PRD default — the 27-row table I showed above).
  - B) Saravali variant as default (it differs in a few rows).
  - C) Both tables shipped as separate rule-YAMLs; BPHS is Josi-default; astrologer can
  override per profile.
  - D) Research — spawn agent to verify BPHS vs Saravali actual deltas and propose.

  ---
  Q4 — Ashtottari starting-lord algorithm.
  - A) Continuous longitude-based (PRD current): cumulative_years = moon_long/360 × 108.
  Matches JH 7.x.
  - B) Discrete nakshatra-bucket per BPHS 47.4-7 verse form (8 clusters of 3+
  nakshatras). Textually closer to BPHS but produces sharp transitions at nakshatra
  boundaries.
  - C) Continuous as default + discrete as alternate source for astrologers who prefer
  verse-literal.

  ---
  Q5 — Dasa hierarchy depth.
  - A) MD + AD + PD — 3 levels (PRD current).
  - B) Add Sookshma (4 levels).
  - C) Add Sookshma + Prana (5 levels).
  - D) 3 default + astrologer requests deeper on demand (compute lazily).

  ---
  Q6 — Rahu/Ketu node type (used across all dasa + transit work, not just E1a).
  Current code uses swe.MEAN_NODE.
  - A) Mean Node (current; classical BPHS, smoother motion).
  - B) True Node (astronomically more accurate, discrete jumps).
  - C) Mean default + astrologer per-profile override.

  ---
  Q7 — Paksha determination for Ashtottari eligibility (if any strict mode picked in Q2).
  - A) Tithi-based: tithi 1-15 shukla, 16-30 krishna. (Panchang standard.)
  - B) Moon-Sun longitude difference: 0-180° shukla, 180-360° krishna.
  - C) Tithi for audit + Sun-Moon longitude as validator; assert equality.

  ---
  Q8 — Nakshatra-boundary edge case (Moon at exact boundary, e.g., 13°20'00.00").
  - A) Boundary-inclusive lower: 0°00'00" ≤ long < 13°20'00" = Ashwini.
  - B) Use Moon's instantaneous speed direction to assign (entering Bharani → Bharani).
  - C) Within 1 arc-second of boundary, flag as ambiguous; require rectification
  suggestion.

  ---
  Q9 — Dasa boundary precision / matching bar vs JH 7.x.
  PRD sets ±1 day.
  - A) ±1 day (PRD current). Sufficient for practical astrology; matches JH rounding.
  - B) ±1 hour. Tighter; catches off-by-timezone bugs earlier.
  - C) ±1 second. Astronomical precision; only achievable if Moon longitude precision is
  also sub-second.

  ---
  Q10 — Dasa balance rendering precision (what we store/show, not compute precision).
  - A) Years as float (e.g., 0.444 yr).
  - B) Years + Months + Days + Hours + Minutes breakdown (classical presentation format,
  common in JH).
  - C) Both — store as float, render in Y/M/D/H/M breakdown.

  ---
  Q11 — Timezone for rendered dasa boundary dates.
  - A) Birthplace timezone always (classical — your chart runs on your birth locale).
  - B) Always UTC + UI localizes per viewer.
  - C) Store UTC canonically + render in birthplace tz; astrologer can toggle.

  ---
  Q12 — Vimshottari refactor risk (the PRD proposes to refactor current
  dasa_calculator.py into a shared BaseDasaEngine).
  This touches already-shipped behavior.
  - A) Refactor with behavior-preserving tests (golden-chart diff against current output
  = bit-exact).
  - B) Don't refactor; duplicate the base logic in new Yogini/Ashtottari engines.
  Slightly more code, zero regression risk.
  - C) Refactor but gate the new engine behind a feature flag for 2 weeks before cutover.

  ---

  your picks to the PRD (updating ## Open Questions Resolved or adding a ## Design
  Decisions section), bump last_updated, and log in review.md. Then we move to E2a.

  Step B — Calculation pipeline: what happens when a chart is computed
                                                                                         
                                                                                         
    Today's pipeline (unchanged by E1a up to step 8):
    1. Birth inputs → geocoding → UT conversion (birthplace tz + DST).
    2. Swiss Ephemeris → tropical planetary positions for all 8 planets + Rahu (Mean     
  Node); Ketu = Rahu + 180°.
    Review: for rahu calculate mean node and true node both. Also for ketu calculate     
  mean node and true node both. When an astrologer is calculating chart, ask this as an  
  input from the astrologer, for the user calculating chart, choose the true node.       
    3. Apply ayanamsa (currently Lahiri, hard-coded in astrology_service.py:56) →        
  sidereal longitudes.
    Review: For an astrologer, give options for raman, lahiri, krichnamurti, true chitra 
   (chitra paksha). What are the other options available to a astrologer, provide me the 
   options after you research. For the user calculating chart, choose lahiri.
    4. Derive rāśi (longitude ÷ 30°) and nakshatra + pada (longitude ÷ 13°20', then ÷    
  3°20').
    5. Compute Lagna using house-system rules (current code uses Whole-Sign rāśi chart + 
   separate Bhava
    Chalit).

  astrologer using the system

  parents, so you should also mention the usage of the chart inside the chart in the     
  middle.
    7. Compute Panchang (tithi, yoga, karana, vaara, nakshatra).
    Review: Also show the english calendar, 60 year cycle name, and the tithi name in    
  sanskrit and english. Sun based month and day.

  ---
  Flag #1 — Vimshottari dasa depth for end-users

  You said "can we do 5 level" about Vimshottari. Technically yes — MD→AD→PD→Sookshma→Prana is a standard
  5-level subdivision.

  But for the end-user default, 5 levels produces periods like "Jupiter-Saturn-Mercury-Rahu-Venus = 2 hours
  14 minutes" — which is overwhelming for a casual B2C user asking "what's my current dasa?"

  Q — Vimshottari depth by user type:

  - A) Both see 5 levels. Deep precision for everyone.
  - B) End-user sees 3 levels (MD+AD+PD); Astrologer sees 5 levels. Compute all 5 always; UI renders per user
   type.
  - C) End-user sees 2 levels (MD+AD only — very accessible); Astrologer sees 5 levels.
  - D) End-user sees 3 levels by default, with an "expand" button to reveal deeper. Astrologer sees 5 levels
  always.
  - E) Other — tell me.

  Which? Once you answer, I'll capture it and move to Flag #2.

  Also — should I spawn the two research agents now (ayanamsa full list + house systems) in parallel in the
  background, so their findings are ready by the time we reach those questions? They run independently and
  don't block this conversation.

  Agent's proposed B2C vs Astrologer split

  B2C "Daily Panchangam" — one-screen default (with location-precise start/end for all time-bound items)

  1. Date header: Gregorian · Vikram Samvat · Shaka Samvat · Samvatsara · Ayana · Ritu · Masa (Amanta + Purnimanta) · Paksha ·       
  Regional solar month (auto-detect by user region)

  3. Sun/Moon: Sunrise, Sunset, Moonrise, Moonset, Day-length
  4. Auspicious windows: Abhijit · Brahma · Amrit Kala · Guru/Ravi Pushya flag · Amrita-Siddhi/Sarvartha-Siddhi flags
  5. Inauspicious windows: Rahu Kala · Yamagandam · Gulika · Dur Muhurta · Varjyam · Bhadra/Vishti

  7. Festival/observance tag (Ekadashi/Pradosha/Sankashti/Purnima/Amavasya/named festival)

                                                                                                                                     ❯ i want to know the sunrise sunset are based on visibilit y of middle  limb and disappearance of middle limb. pl clarify. with        middle limb of sun give sample again. again as per saura masa today is chithirai day 6 and not 7 pl                                
                                                                                                                                     
● Good catches on both.                                                                                                              
                                          
  Clarification: sunrise/sunset conventions                                                                                             
  "Middle limb" = center of the Sun's disc at horizon. Four conventions exist in ephemeris software:                                                                                                                                                                      
  ┌─────┬───────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │  #  │        Convention         │ Where the Sun's center is at "sunrise" │                     Used by                      │      ├─────┼───────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ 1   │ Upper limb at horizon,    │ Center ~50' below horizon (34'         │ Swiss Eph default · Western geographical ·       │
  │     │ with refraction           │ refraction + 16' half-radius)          │ weather forecasts                                │    
  ├─────┼───────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤    
  │     │ Center of disc at         │ Center at apparent horizon (refraction │ Modern Indian panchang — Drik panchang,          │    
  │ 2   │ horizon, with refraction  │  corrects back to true horizon)        │ mypanchang, Kalnirnay, most published Indian     │    
  │     │                           │                                        │ almanacs                                         │    
  ├─────┼───────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤    
  │     │ Center of disc at         │                                        │                                                  │    
  │ 3   │ horizon, without          │ Center at true geometric horizon       │ Strict Surya Siddhanta / Siddhantic tradition    │    
  │     │ refraction                │                                        │                                                  │    
  ├─────┼───────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ 4   │ Lower limb at horizon     │ Center 16' above horizon               │ Uncommon                                         │    
  └─────┴───────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    

  You asked for middle limb = center. That maps to #2 or #3. Delta between them: ~2 minutes (refraction lifts the Sun ~34
  arc-minutes, which at Sun's horizon rate crosses the horizon in ~2 min).

  Swiss Ephemeris flag to use: SE_BIT_DISC_CENTER (256), optionally combined with SE_BIT_NO_REFRACTION (512) for #3.

  ---
  Flag — sunrise/sunset convention (both user types — need your pick)

  - A) #2 — Center of disc + refraction. Matches Drik panchang, mypanchang, Kalnirnay, standard Indian published almanacs. Same for  
  both user types. ← my recommendation; matches what Indian users see elsewhere.
  - B) #3 — Center of disc, no refraction (strict Siddhantic). Diverges from published almanacs by ~2 min; astrologers who
  cross-check against Drik panchang will flag the mismatch.
  - C) End-user sees #2; Astrologer configurable between #2 / #3 / #1 (Western-compatible).
  - D) Kerala Vakya / Parahita school uses a different convention — flag as a 4th option if needed. (If you want me to research this,
   say "research.")

  Which?

  → 19 April = Chithirai Day 6. ✓ (my earlier "Day 7" was off-by-one — fixed.)

  ── DATE HEADER (corrected) ─────────────────────────────────────────

  ── SUN / MOON — with sunrise/sunset definition annotation ──────────
  Convention       : Center of Sun's disc at apparent horizon,
                     with atmospheric refraction (Drik panchang /
                     modern Indian almanac standard)
                     Swiss Eph flag: SE_BIT_DISC_CENTER

  Sunrise          : 05:55:28 IST    (center of disc at horizon)
                     (cf. geographic upper-limb sunrise: 05:54:23,
                      Δ = +1m 05s)
  Sunset           : 18:23:10 IST    (center of disc at horizon)
                     (cf. geographic upper-limb sunset: 18:24:15,
                      Δ = −1m 05s)
  Moonrise         : 07:13:53 IST    (center of Moon's disc)
  Moonset          : 20:44:17 IST    (center of Moon's disc)
  Day length       : 12h 27m 42s     (sunset − sunrise, center-based)
  Night length     : 11h 32m 18s
  Madhyahna (Hindu noon, = sunrise + day_length/2)
                   : 12:09:19 IST

  ---
  Downstream impact (for record — all Sun-derived windows shift with sunrise):
  - Brahma Muhurta: 04:19:16 – 05:55:28 (was 04:18:11 – 05:54:23)
  - Abhijit Muhurta: 11:45:30 – 12:33:08 (~same, centered on Madhyahna)
  - All 30 Muhurtas shift forward ~1 min
  - Choghadiya day periods shift forward ~1 min; day length 1m 05s shorter → each ~94-min slot shrinks by ~8s
  - Rahu Kala / Yamagandam / Gulika Kala: shift forward ~1 min; each slot ~8s shorter
  - 24 Hora starts at center-disc sunrise
  - Tara Bala / Chandra Bala: nakshatra/sign position at sunrise shifts slightly (Moon moves ~0.5°/hour, so ~0.5 arc-min over 1 min —
   negligible but technically present)

  Same correction applied throughout the sample if you approve #2.

❯ if A is the choice what is sunrise time and sunset time                                                                                                                                      
● If Option A (center of disc + refraction) is chosen, for Chennai on 19 April 2026:                                                 

  Sunrise : 05:55:28 IST   (center of disc at apparent horizon, refraction corrected)                                                  Sunset  : 18:23:10 IST   (center of disc at apparent horizon, refraction corrected)
                                                                                                                                       Cross-check vs other conventions (same date, same location):                                                                           Upper limb + refraction (Swiss Eph default) : 05:54:23 sunrise · 18:24:15 sunset                                                     Center of disc + refraction    (Option A)   : 05:55:28 sunrise · 18:23:10 sunset                                                     Center of disc, no refraction  (Option B)   : 05:57:20 sunrise · 18:21:18 sunset                                                 

  Approximate deltas from upper-limb (SE default):
  - Center + refraction: sunrise ~+65 sec later, sunset ~−65 sec earlier
  - Center, no refraction: sunrise ~+3 min later, sunset ~−3 min earlier

  Reasoning (geometry): Sun's apparent vertical rate near horizon at Chennai latitude ≈ 0.25°/min = 15'/min. Sun's half-radius = 16'.
   Refraction at horizon = 34'. So:
  - From "upper limb appears" to "center at horizon" = 16' = ~64 seconds (both with refraction)
  - From "upper limb appears" to "center at true horizon" (no refraction) = 16' + 34' = 50' = ~3 min 20 sec

  Caveat: these are to-the-second projections from the geometry, assuming atmospheric refraction constant = 34' (IAU standard).      
  Actual Swiss Ephemeris computation with SE_BIT_DISC_CENTER | SEFLG_SWIEPH will produce the authoritative values, which may differ  
  by ±2–5 seconds based on altitude, actual local atmospheric pressure/temperature if supplied. If you want me to run the real Swiss 
  Ephemeris computation for a specific chart to confirm, I can spawn a task to execute Python in the docker container.

  Still waiting on your pick — Flag on sunrise convention: A / B / C / D.

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────┬────────────┬─────────────┐
  │                      Convention                      │ Sunrise IST │ Sunset IST │ Day length  │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼────────────┼─────────────┤

  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼────────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Option A — Center + refraction (Drik panchang)       │ 05:55:28    │ 18:23:10   │ 12h 27m 42s │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────┼────────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Option B — Center, no refraction (strict Siddhantic) │ 05:57:47    │ 18:20:51   │ 12h 23m 04s │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────┴────────────┴─────────────┘

  Delta A vs B: Option B sunrise is ~2 min 19 sec later than A; Option B sunset is ~2 min 19 sec earlier than A. Day length is ~4 min
   38 sec shorter under B.

  Why the delta: refraction lifts the Sun's apparent position by ~34 arc-minutes at horizon. With refraction disabled, the Sun must  
  rise an additional 34' after it would have already cleared an apparent horizon — at Chennai's Sun-rise rate of ~14.7'/min, that's  
  2m 19s per side.

  Impact on downstream timings if you pick B instead of A: all sunrise-anchored windows (Brahma Muhurta, Abhijit, 30 muhurtas,       
  Choghadiya, Hora, Rahu Kala, Yamaganda, Gulika) shift forward by ~2m 19s. Day/night boundary shifts in both places.

  Practical implication for cross-verification: most published Indian panchangs (Drik, mypanchang, Kalnirnay, Tamil almanacs) match  
  Option A to within 10–30 seconds. Under Option B, your sunrise will be 2+ minutes later than what users see in those sources —     
  which may trigger "why does Josi's sunrise not match Drik panchang?" support questions.

  Caveat: values are to-the-second projections from standard-atmosphere geometry (34' refraction at horizon). Authoritative values   
  come from Swiss Ephemeris at runtime with SE_BIT_DISC_CENTER [| SE_BIT_NO_REFRACTION], and may vary by ±2–5 seconds with local     
  pressure/temperature corrections. I can run a live Python Swiss Eph computation inside the Docker container if you want exact      
  values — say "run it" and I'll do so.

  Q — First implementation nuance: Samvatsara numbering

  The 60-year Samvatsara (Jovian) cycle has two numbering conventions — results in different Samvatsara names for the same Gregorian year depending on tradition.    

  Example for 2026:
  - South Indian (Prabhava-start, Andhra/Telugu/Kannada/Tamil) — Chaitra 2026 Samvatsara = Vishwavasu (cycle position 39)
  - North Indian (Barhaspatya cycle, offset ~22 years earlier) — Chaitra 2026 Samvatsara = Siddharthi (cycle position 54)

  Josi needs to pick one default per user type.

  Q:

  - A) South-Indian Prabhava-start for both user types (matches Drik panchang default, Tamil/Telugu/Kannada/Malayalam users). Astrologer can toggle to North-Indian. 
  - B) North-Indian Barhaspatya for both user types (matches North Indian regional almanacs). Astrologer can toggle.
  - C) End-user: auto-detect by region (South users → Prabhava-start; North/Hindi users → Barhaspatya). Astrologer: both shown side-by-side.
  - D) Show BOTH names to both user types always (format: "Vishwavasu (South) / Siddharthi (North)").
  - E) Research — I want to see which is more widely cited in classical Parashari sources before deciding.

  Which?
● Samvatsara numbering locked: Option A — South-Indian Prabhava-start default for both user types. Astrologer can toggle to North-Indian Barhaspatya cycle. Current  
  cycle position: Vishwavasu (39) for Chaitra 2026.

  Q — Next nuance: Nakshatra-percentile table source (drives Varjyam, Amrit Kala, Dur Muhurta, and several yoga detections)                                                                                                                                                                                                                 Varjyam, Amrit Kala, and related windows are computed as specific percentile ranges of each nakshatra's duration. Example: "Ashwini Varjyam is the 50.0–56.25%       window" (i.e., the 8th-to-9th ghati inside Ashwini). Different classical texts give slightly different percentile tables.                                                                                                                                                                                                               
  Four classical sources with published tables:

  - Muhurta Chintamani (Rama Daivajna, 16th c.) — most widely cited in modern panchangs; standard for Drik Panchang, mypanchang.com, Kalnirnay
  - Narada Samhita — older source; minor percentile shifts for some nakshatras (difference: ~5–15 min per window)
  - Kalaprakashika — Kerala regional variant; used by Malayalam Panchangam (Kollam-era calendars)
  - Brihat Samhita (Varahamihira) — oldest available; sparse on specific percentile values; often supplemented from Muhurta Chintamani

  Q — Source for nakshatra-percentile tables (Varjyam, Amrit Kala, Dur Muhurta, shared yoga detector):

  - A) Muhurta Chintamani as default for both user types. Matches Drik Panchang / Kalnirnay / mypanchang — published almanac cross-verification works cleanly.       
  - B) Muhurta Chintamani for end-user; astrologer can toggle between Muhurta Chintamani / Narada Samhita / Kalaprakashika / Brihat Samhita (auxiliary source picker,
   like ayanamsha selector).
  - C) Kalaprakashika for Malayalam-region users (to match Kerala Panchangam); Muhurta Chintamani for everyone else. Astrologer toggle available.
  - D) Both Muhurta Chintamani + Narada Samhita computed always (two sibling rules); aggregation strategy picks the "confidence-weighted" default (B from the master 
  design) — shows user a single window with confidence annotation.
  - E) Research — spawn an agent to verify whether the four sources actually differ materially, then come back to decide.

  Which?

  │             │ Ch.5). All four sources give the same weekday table.                                                │ non-applicability of percentile framing) │

  │ Amrit Kala  │ Material variance exists. Placement offset from Varjya varies by up to ~95 min between Muhurta      │ Material — needs decision                │   
  │             │ Chintamani (+8 ghatis after Varjya start) and Prashna Marga / Kalaprakashika lineages.              │                                          │   
  └─────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────┘   

  Modern-tool convergence: Drik Panchang, mypanchang.com, Kalnirnay, Jagannatha Hora all use Muhurta Chintamani. Kerala publishers use Kalaprakashika but Varjya     
  tables are numerically identical.

  Agent's recommendation: Use Muhurta Chintamani as default for all three. No multi-source aggregation needed. Kerala users' perceived differences come
  overwhelmingly from Vakya vs Drik siddhanta for sunrise (15–40 min delta), not from the percentile table — that's the bigger decision to address separately.

  Sub-flag surfaced by the research — Amrit Kala placement offset

  This is the ONE genuine disagreement: where does Amrit Kala sit relative to Varjya?

  - Muhurta Chintamani lineage: Amrit Kala starts 8 ghatis after Varjya start (i.e., immediately after Varjya ends, same 4-ghati duration). Used by Drik Panchang,   
  mypanchang, Kalnirnay, JH.
  - Prashna Marga / Kalaprakashika lineage: Different offset, producing a ~95 min earlier or later Amrit Kala window. Used by some Kerala publishers.

  - X) Use Muhurta Chintamani offset for both user types (matches all modern panchangs).
  - Y) Expose both as astrologer options; MC as default.
  - Z) Defer until we explicitly address Vakya/Drik siddhanta split (the bigger Kerala-vs-Pan-India decision).

  ---                                                                                                                                                                  Q — Next Panchangam nuance: Siddhanta school (Drik vs Vakya)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  This was flagged by the last research agent as the bigger Kerala-vs-Pan-India decision — 15–40 min divergence in sunrise and planetary positions. Two (actually
  three) classical computation schools:

  ┌─────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   
  │           School            │                                   Method                                   │                      Used by                      │   
  ├─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Drik Siddhanta (दृक् /        │ Astronomical / observational — uses real-time ephemeris. Swiss Ephemeris   │ Modern Indian panchangs (Drik Panchang,           │ 
  │ "observed")                 │ is inherently Drik.                                                        │ mypanchang), North Indian tradition, Jagannatha   │   
  │                             │                                                                            │ Hora, most Parashari software                     │   
  ├─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Vakya Siddhanta (वाक्य /      │ Fixed-formula computation from Kali Dhruva (positions at Kali Yuga epoch,  │ Kerala Panchangam (Malayalam almanacs),           │ 
  │ "sentence-formula")         │ 18 Feb 3102 BCE) + incremental algorithms from Surya Siddhanta,            │ traditional Tamil Nadu Vakya panchangs, some      │   
  │                             │ Grahalaghava, or Vakya-Karana.                                             │ Andhra regional publishers                        │   
  ├─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Parahita Siddhanta          │ Hybrid — refined by Haridatta in 683 CE (Grahacara-nibandhana) for Kerala; │ Older Kerala tradition, some Odia panchangs       │   
  │                             │  sits between Drik and strict Vakya.                                       │                                                   │   
  └─────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   

  Impact of Drik vs Vakya:
  - Sunrise/sunset: 15–40 min delta at same location
  - Nakshatra transitions: 5–20 min delta
  - Tithi transitions: similar
  - Planet longitudes: can be 1–5 arc-minutes off (meaningful for muhurta, degree-sensitive yogas)
  - Sankranti dates: can shift by ~1 day in edge cases

  Engineering reality:
  - Josi currently = Drik (inherited from Swiss Ephemeris).
  - Adding Vakya support = building a separate computation engine (Surya-Siddhanta-based or Vakya-Karana-based — substantial effort).

  Q — Siddhanta for Panchangam + chart:

   software. Document clearly in user-facing docs.
  - B) Drik default for both user types; Vakya as astrologer-profile option (build Vakya engine as P2 or P3 roadmap item). When a Kerala astrologer toggles Vakya,   
  all panchang + chart calculations rebase to Vakya.
  - C) Drik default for B2C; Parahita as astrologer option — Parahita is a Kerala middle-ground and easier to implement than strict Vakya.
  - D) Drik + Vakya both computed always; astrologer-mode shows side-by-side divergence (diagnostic value); B2C sees Drik only.
  - E) Research — how big is the Kerala/traditional-Vakya user base in Josi's target market, and what do competitors (astrogold, Parashara's Light, JH) do for Kerala
   users? Decide after.

  Q — Next Panchangam nuance: Panchaka subtypes                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
  Panchaka = 5-nakshatra inauspicious cluster when Moon transits Dhanishta (2nd half) → Shatabhisha → Purva Bhadrapada → Uttara Bhadrapada → Revati (~5 days every     ~27 days).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
  Classical tradition defines 5 subtypes based on the weekday during which Panchaka is active — each with a specific inauspicious-matter warning:

  ┌───────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   
  │  Weekday  │                         Subtype                          │                                   Classical warning                                   │   
  ├───────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Sunday    │ Agni Panchaka (अग्नि)                                      │ Avoid fire-work: cooking experiments, welding, kindling new flame, fire rituals       │ 
  ├───────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Monday    │ Raja Panchaka (राज)                                       │ Avoid authority/legal matters: court cases, government filings, petitioning           │  
  ├───────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Tuesday   │ Mrityu Panchaka (मृत्यु)                                    │ Avoid risk exposure: surgery start, dangerous journey, quarrels                       │
  ├───────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Wednesday │ (neutral — no Panchaka subtype; some sources say         │ —                                                                                     │   
  │           │ "Subha")                                                 │                                                                                       │   
  ├───────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Thursday  │ (neutral — no Panchaka subtype)                          │ —                                                                                     │   
  ├───────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Friday    │ Chora Panchaka (चोर)                                      │ Avoid valuables exposure: financial transactions, leaving house unlocked, jewelry     │  
  │           │                                                          │ wear                                                                                  │   
  ├───────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Saturday  │ Roga Panchaka (रोग)                                       │ Avoid health-risk activities: surgery, new treatment, long fasting                    │  
  └───────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   

  Classical source: Muhurta Chintamani Ch.4 + Narada Samhita; convergent across traditions.

  Q — Panchaka computation + surfacing:

  - A) Compute Panchaka active (boolean) + subtype name + classical warning string. Both user types see all three. Warning text is surfaced in B2C as a caution      
  badge.
  - B) B2C sees only "Panchaka active" flag with a generic message ("traditional caution period today"). Astrologer sees subtype + full classical warning.
  - C) Astrologer-only — Panchaka is too esoteric for B2C; don't surface to end-user at all. Compute and store, expose only via Workbench.
  - D) Both user types see subtype + warning. Add a "how significant is this" severity indicator (low/medium/high) per subtype based on classical weight (Mrityu >   
  Chora/Roga > Agni/Raja).
  - E) Other — tell me.

  Which?

❯ D

  ┌───────────────────────────┬──────────┐                                                                                                                             │          Subtype          │ Severity │                                                                                                                           
  ├───────────────────────────┼──────────┤                                                                                                                             │ Mrityu Panchaka (Tuesday) │   High   │                                                                                                                             ├───────────────────────────┼──────────┤                                                                                                                             │ Chora Panchaka (Friday)   │  Medium  │                                                                                                                             ├───────────────────────────┼──────────┤                                                                                                                           
  │ Roga Panchaka (Saturday)  │  Medium  │
  ├───────────────────────────┼──────────┤
  │ Agni Panchaka (Sunday)    │   Low    │
  ├───────────────────────────┼──────────┤
  │ Raja Panchaka (Monday)    │   Low    │
  └───────────────────────────┴──────────┘

  Wednesday and Thursday Panchaka days = no subtype, displayed as "Panchaka active (neutral — no weekday subtype)."

  ---
  Q — Next Panchangam nuance: Masa convention primary for B2C display

  Chandra Masa (lunar month) has two regional naming conventions that give different month-names for the same calendar day:

  ┌─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     Convention      │ Month = new-moon-to-new-moon / full-moon-to-full-moon │                                Used by                                │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Amanta (अमान्त)       │ New-moon to new-moon                                  │ South India (Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, Malayalam), Gujarat, Maharashtra │
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Purnimanta (पूर्णिमान्त) │ Full-moon to full-moon                                │ North India (Hindi belt, Bihar, UP, MP, Rajasthan, Punjab), Nepal     │       
  └─────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Example — the name difference is exactly one month offset in Krishna Paksha:
  - Today (April 19, 2026): Chaitra under Amanta (South) / Vaishakha under Purnimanta (North)
  - Holi night (full moon Phalguna/Chaitra) falls in: Phalguna Purnima under Amanta / Chaitra Purnima under Purnimanta
  - Maha Shivaratri: Magha Krishna 14 under Amanta / Phalguna Krishna 14 under Purnimanta

  Shukla Paksha names match across both systems; only Krishna Paksha masa-names shift by one.

  Per the locked B2C Option D (regional auto-adapt), masa convention should follow user region. But need to confirm default behavior and astrologer options.

  Q — Masa convention for B2C + astrologer:

  - A) Auto-adapt by user region — South/Gujarat/Maharashtra users see Amanta primary; North/Hindi-belt users see Purnimanta primary. Both computed always; B2C      
  surfaces one. Astrologer sees both side-by-side always.
  - B) Always show both to everyone: "Chaitra (Amanta) / Vaishakha (Purnimanta)". No regional adaptation.
  - C) Amanta default for all B2C (majority of Indian pop uses Amanta when you include Tamil/Telugu/Kannada/Malayalam/Gujarati/Marathi). Astrologer can toggle.      
  - D) Purnimanta default for all B2C (aligns with classical BPHS which uses Purnimanta). Astrologer can toggle.
  - E) Research — spawn agent to verify actual regional population split + which convention dominates in each target market before deciding.

  Which?

  always; B2C surfaces one. Astrologer sees both side-by-side regardless.

  ---
  Q — Nakshatra count: 27 vs 28 (Abhijit inclusion)

  Classical tradition has two parallel conventions:

  ┌────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    
  │   Convention   │          Nakshatras           │                   Each spans                   │                           Notes                           │    
  ├────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ 27-count       │ Ashwini → Revati              │ 13°20' each                                    │ Dominant in daily panchang, Vimshottari dasa, all         │    
  │ (standard)     │                               │                                                │ nakshatra-based computations                              │    
  ├────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ 28-count (with │ Ashwini → Revati + Abhijit    │ ~12°51' each on average; Abhijit spans ~4°13'  │ Used in muhurta computations (Abhijit as "safe bridge"),  │    
  │  Abhijit)      │ inserted between U.Ashadha    │ (last quarter of U.Ashadha + first 1/15 of     │ Sarvatobhadra Chakra (the 9×9 grid literally needs 28),   │    
  │                │ and Shravana                  │ Shravana, ~276°40' to 280°53')                 │ some classical muhurta texts                              │    
  └────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    

  Important distinction: "Abhijit Muhurta" (the ~48-min time-window around solar noon, 8th muhurta of day) is different from "Abhijit Nakshatra" (the 28th lunar     
  mansion). They're unrelated except for sharing the auspicious-bridge theme. Don't conflate.

  Classical Parashari position:
  - BPHS mentions Abhijit in muhurta context, not in daily panchang usage
  - Muhurta Chintamani recognizes Abhijit as 28th for muhurta checks
  - Drik Panchang, mypanchang, Kalnirnay: 27-count for daily panchang; 28-count when computing Sarvatobhadra or specific muhurta checks (context-dependent)

  Calculation impact of switching 27 → 28:
  - Each nakshatra's duration shortens ~4.5%
  - Nakshatra boundaries for percentile tables (Varjyam, Amrit Kala) need regeneration under 28-mode
  - Vimshottari dasa starting lord changes for Moon near the U.Ashadha/Shravana boundary (since the "Abhijit zone" was part of U.Ashadha in 27-count but becomes its 
  own in 28-count)
  - Panchaka, Ganda Moola, Ravi Yoga all use 27-count classical tables — switching to 28 would require extending tables (no classical source does this)

  Q — Nakshatra-count convention:

  - A) 27-count for everything. Abhijit never recognized as nakshatra. "Abhijit Muhurta" (time-window) is still computed (it's time-based, not nakshatra-based).     
  - B) Context-dependent (27 + 28 coexist). 27-count for: daily panchang, nakshatra-of-Moon, Vimshottari dasa, percentile tables (Varjyam/Amrit Kala), Panchaka,     
  Ganda Moola. 28-count for: Sarvatobhadra Chakra (required by the 9×9 grid), Abhijit-Muhurta's relationship to Abhijit-Nakshatra crossings. This matches Drik/MC/JH 
  modern convention. ← my recommendation
  - C) 28-count for everything. Extend all classical tables (Panchaka, Ravi Yoga, Ganda Moola) with Abhijit-adjusted values — but no classical source publishes these
   extensions, so Josi would be authoring them.
  - D) User-type split: B2C sees 27 only; Astrologer sees 27 primary + 28-mode toggle for muhurta work.
  - E) Research — verify how Josi's existing enhanced_nakshatra_calculator.py currently handles this before deciding.

  Which?

✻ Cogitated for 37s

❯ A

● Nakshatra count locked: Option A — 27-count for everything. Abhijit not recognized as nakshatra. "Abhijit Muhurta" (time-window) still computed independently      
  (time-based, not nakshatra-based).

  ---
  Sub-flag surfaced by this decision — Sarvatobhadra Chakra (SBC) conflict

  E7's Sarvatobhadra Chakra is classically a 9×9 grid with 28 nakshatras (including Abhijit). It's architecturally built around 28 by construction — you can't fit 27
   into the 9×9 layout without changing the grid geometry.

  Your Option A says "27 for everything." This conflicts with SBC's classical structure. Two ways to resolve:

  Q — SBC exception to Option A?

  - X) A applies strictly — SBC is re-designed to work with 27 nakshatras (non-classical; no source specifies this layout). Effectively: Josi does not ship a        
  classically-correct SBC.
  - Y) A applies to all nakshatra-based computations EXCEPT SBC. SBC retains its classical 28-nakshatra grid because the instrument requires it by construction. All 
  other panchang/chart/muhurta/dasa/yoga work uses 27.
  - Z) Defer SBC decision to E7 PRD review. Lock A now for everything else (panchang + natal chart); revisit SBC as a sub-decision in E7.

  Which?

❯ Y

● SBC exception locked: Option Y — 27-count for all nakshatra-based computations (daily panchang, Vimshottari, percentile tables, Panchaka, Ganda Moola, Ravi Yoga,  
  natal chart). SBC retains its classical 28-nakshatra grid by structural necessity; flagged as a scoped exception in E7 PRD.

  ---
  Q — Ghati definition

  Classical tradition actually has three context-dependent ghati definitions. The choice affects all time-unit computations (muhurta, hora, Varjyam, Amrit Kala      
  windows, Choghadiya boundaries).

  ┌─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    
  │     Ghati type      │                      Definition                       │                                   Used for                                    │    
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ Day-ghati /         │ day_length / 30 (day), night_length / 30 (night) —    │ Muhurta divisions, Hora, Varjyam/Amrit Kala/Dur Muhurta time conversion,      │    
  │ Night-ghati         │ variable with latitude + season                       │ Choghadiya, Rahu Kala, all sunrise-anchored windows                           │    
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ Nakshatra-ghati     │ nakshatra_duration / 60 — variable per nakshatra      │ Nakshatra-internal percentile tables (the 50–54th ghati of Ashwini, etc.)     │    
  │                     │ (~23.4–27.0 min)                                      │                                                                               │    
  ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤    
  │ Solar-day ghati     │ 1440 min / 60 = 24 min fixed                          │ Modern simplified display; not classical                                      │    
  └─────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    

  Most modern Indian panchangs (Drik, mypanchang, Kalnirnay, JH) use the classical contextual approach — day/night-ghati for time-window computations,
  nakshatra-ghati for percentile tables. This is also what Muhurta Chintamani prescribes.

  Q — Ghati definition policy:

  - A) Classical contextual — day-ghati for sunrise-anchored windows (muhurta / hora / Choghadiya / Rahu Kala / Varjyam clock-time), nakshatra-ghati for
  nakshatra-internal percentile tables. Matches Muhurta Chintamani + all modern Indian panchangs. Same for both user types. ← my recommendation
  - B) Fixed 24-min ghatis everywhere. Simpler math; loses classical accuracy; produces values that won't match published panchangs.
  - C) Classical contextual for both user types + expose a "ghati precision" diagnostic for astrologer (shows nakshatra-duration variability for current chart).     
  - D) Other — tell me.

  Which?

  Q — Ravi Yoga nakshatra-count positions

  Ravi Yoga (रवि योग) is an auspicious combination in the auspicious-yoga detector (item #9 in build priority). Definition: activated when Moon is in specific       
  nakshatra positions counted from Sun's nakshatra. Classical effect: nullifies certain inauspicious combinations; favorable for starting activities.

  Sources disagree on exact position set:

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   
  │                  Source                   │                                     Active Moon-positions from Sun-nakshatra                                     │   
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Muhurta Chintamani (Rama Daivajna, 16th   │ 4, 6, 9, 10, 13, 20                                                                                              │   
  │ c.)                                       │                                                                                                                  │   
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Nirnaya Sindhu (Kamalakara Bhatta, 17th   │ 4, 6, 9, 10, 13, 20 (same as MC)                                                                                 │   
  │ c.)                                       │                                                                                                                  │   
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Some older commentaries                   │ 4, 6, 9, 10, 13, 20 and 12 (7-position variant)                                                                  │   
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Drik Panchang modern                      │ 4, 6, 9, 10, 13, 20 (MC standard)                                                                                │   
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Inverse-direction variant                 │ Same positions but counted from Moon's nakshatra to Sun's (uncommon in modern practice but attested in a few     │   
  │                                           │ older texts)                                                                                                     │   
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   

  Related but distinct yogas (each with its own activation rule):
  - Ravi Pushya Yoga = Sunday + Pushya nakshatra (very specific, widely-recognized)
  - Guru Pushya Yoga = Thursday + Pushya nakshatra
  - Ravi Yoga (this one) = broader nakshatra-position rule

  ---
  Q — Dwipushkar / Tripushkar activation tables

  Both are auspicious-yoga detector rules. Activation = specific tithi × vaara × nakshatra intersection.

  Classical activation (Muhurta Chintamani, most widely cited):

  Dwipushkar Yoga — "doubling yoga" (multiplies effects 2×):
  - Tithis: 2, 7, 12 (Dvitiya, Saptami, Dvadasi — "Bhadra" tithis)
  - Vaaras: Sunday, Tuesday, Saturday
  - Nakshatras: Bharani, Krittika, Uttara Phalguni, Uttara Ashadha, Uttara Bhadrapada (dwi-swabhava / shared-deity set)
  - Activation: ALL THREE must match simultaneously

  Tripushkar Yoga — "tripling yoga" (multiplies effects 3×):
  - Tithis: 2, 7, 12 (same)
  - Vaaras: Sunday, Tuesday, Saturday (same)
  - Nakshatras: Punarvasu, Uttara Phalguni, Vishakha, Uttara Ashadha, Krittika (different set — "tripushkar" nakshatras, partial overlap with Dwipushkar)
  - Activation: ALL THREE must match simultaneously (mutually exclusive with Dwipushkar when nakshatra doesn't overlap)

  Source variance:
  - MC, Nirnaya Sindhu, Drik Panchang agree on the tables above
  - A few regional commentaries show minor nakshatra substitutions (~1 nakshatra difference); materially rare
  - Practical Dwipushkar activations per year: 8-15 days; Tripushkar: 6-12 days

  Q — Dwipushkar/Tripushkar activation:

  - A) MC standard tables (as above). Single rule. Same for both user types.
  - B) MC default + astrologer variant toggle (regional commentaries). Same pattern as Ravi Yoga.
  - C) MC default for both. Additionally, astrologer sees a "near-miss" diagnostic — when 2 of the 3 conditions match (tithi + vaara but wrong nakshatra, etc.), flag
   as "Dwipushkar-adjacent" for muhurta planning context.
  - D) Research — verify nakshatra sets against primary MC manuscript before locking.
  - E) Other.

  Which?

✻ Cooked for 31s

❯ D
  Explain this more

● Dwipushkar & Tripushkar Yogas — deeper explanation

  Name origin. Pushkara = "lotus / blessed / amplifying container." Dwi-pushkar = "two-fold amplifier." Tri-pushkar = "three-fold amplifier."

  Classical nature. These are karaṇī yogas — they don't add goodness or badness themselves; they multiply whatever action you start during the window. The classical 
  texts are explicit about this:

  - Start a benefic activity during Dwipushkar → the benefit doubles.
  - Start a malefic activity during Dwipushkar → the loss doubles.
  - Tripushkar triples in either direction.

  This is why they are muhurta tools, not daily auspiciousness flags. Classical astrologers use them deliberately — pick the activity carefully, then pick the       
  window. Not "everything done now is great."

  Practical use in modern Parashari muhurta:

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     Recommended during Dwi/Tripushkar     │                             Avoided during Dwi/Tripushkar                             │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Buying gold, silver, precious metals      │ Taking loans (debt doubles/triples)                                                   │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Starting investments, fixed deposits      │ Starting litigation as plaintiff (conflict escalates)                                 │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Signing long-term contracts               │ Initiating any conflict or hostile action                                             │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Opening a new shop / launching a business │ Starting medical diagnosis in serious illness (fear of negative diagnosis amplifying) │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Inheritance/property registration         │ Lending money at high risk                                                            │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Betrothal (sagai), marriage arrangements  │ Making one-time losses acceptable                                                     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Three-condition activation (why they're rare):

  Tithi (2, 7, 12) × Vaara (Sun, Tue, Sat) × Nakshatra (specific 5 set) — all three must align simultaneously. This restricts activations to 8–15 days per year for  
  Dwipushkar, 6–12 days for Tripushkar. Rarity is part of their muhurta value.

  Why the nakshatra set specifically matters:

  - Dwipushkar nakshatras (Bharani, Krittika, U.Phalguni, U.Ashadha, U.Bhadrapada) classically share the theme of "ubhayadaivata" — dual-deity or shared-ownership   
  nakshatras. Duality → doubling. Classical symbolic logic.
  - Tripushkar nakshatras (Punarvasu, U.Phalguni, Vishakha, U.Ashadha, Krittika) classically share a three-fold / triangular quality; their deity groupings are      
  traditionally associated with tripling motifs (Vishakha = dual deity with three paces; U.Phalguni = dual-phalguni in a three-part cycle). Classical symbolic logic 
  again.

  Why research is worth doing here (what option D will verify):

  1. Whether the exact nakshatra sets I listed match primary Muhurta Chintamani manuscript (I'm citing general-knowledge/Drik-Panchang-level info, not the Sanskrit  
  primary text).
  2. Whether Kalaprakashika (Kerala source) differs on the 5-nakshatra list — if yes, Malayalam users would see different Dwipushkar dates than Tamil/North users.   
  3. Whether Nirnaya Sindhu (major 17th-c Muhurta compendium, widely followed) agrees.
  4. Whether any classical source adds or removes nakshatras — even one substitution shifts 2-3 activation days per year, which matters for muhurta.
  5. Confirm the tithi set (2, 7, 12) — I believe correct but unverified against primary text; some commentaries extend to include tithi 3 or swap 2→3 per regional  
  practice.
  6. Confirm the vaara set (Sun/Tue/Sat) — standard but similarly unverified against primary text.

  Research plan: spawn agent to verify all three activation conditions against primary Muhurta Chintamani + Nirnaya Sindhu + Kalaprakashika + modern Drik Panchang;  
  report any source-to-source deltas; recommend a single default set for Josi.

  Spawning now? Say yes and I'll kick it off in background (same pattern as the previous research agents), or adjust the brief first if you want.

  ---
  Q — Hora (planetary hour) computation variant

  Hora = planetary-hour division. Day + night each divided into 12 segments, each ruled by a planet. Used for elective muhurta (start a journey in Jupiter-hora,     
  money-work in Venus-hora, surgery in Saturn-hora avoided, etc.).

  Classical standard (Muhurta Chintamani / Brihat Samhita / most modern panchangs):
  - Day (sunrise to sunset) divided into 12 equal segments → 12 day-horas. Duration = day_length / 12 (variable with latitude + season, ~60 min average).
  - Night (sunset to sunrise) divided into 12 equal segments → 12 night-horas. Duration = night_length / 12.
  - First hora of day = day-lord (vaara lord). Sunday → Sun-hora first. Monday → Moon-hora first. Etc.
  - Subsequent horas follow Chaldean order (planets by orbital period, slowest to fastest, reversed when traversing): Sa → Ju → Ma → Su → Ve → Me → Mo → Sa → Ju →   
  ...
  - After 24 horas (full day + night), the 25th hora = next day's day-lord, which correctly rolls over the vaara cycle.

  Worked example for Sunday:
  - Day Horas (12): Sun, Venus, Mercury, Moon, Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Sun, Venus, Mercury, Moon, Saturn
  - Night Horas (12): Jupiter, Mars, Sun, Venus, Mercury, Moon, Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Sun, Venus, Mercury
  - 25th hora (next day sunrise) = Moon → Monday ✓

  Variants in circulation:

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   
  │               Variant               │                           Description                           │                       Used by                        │   
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Chaldean + variable-length          │ As described above                                              │ Muhurta Chintamani · modern Indian panchangs · Drik  │   
  │ (standard)                          │                                                                 │ · JH                                                 │   
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Kala Hora (fixed 60-min)            │ 60-min hours starting at sunrise, ignoring day/night length     │ Some regional / simplified practice                  │   
  │                                     │ variation                                                       │                                                      │   
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Day-hora only                       │ Only 12 daytime horas computed; no night horas                  │ Some muhurta manuals focused on daytime activities   │   
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Reversed Chaldean                   │ Same lords but reversed sequence                                │ Rare; unattested in main classical texts             │   
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤   
  │ Vara-lord-only                      │ Day-lord rules ALL 24 horas (no rotation)                       │ Popular/folk simplification; non-classical           │   
  └─────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   

  Q — Hora computation:

  - A) Chaldean + variable-length for both user types. Day_length/12 and night_length/12. Standard per MC and all modern panchangs. Same for both.
  - B) Chaldean standard default + astrologer toggle to Kala Hora (fixed 60-min) for cross-verification with simplified regional panchangs.
  - C) Chaldean standard default + astrologer toggle exposes all variants (Kala Hora, day-hora-only, reversed Chaldean) for comparative/research use.
  - D) Kala Hora (fixed 60-min) default — simpler rendering, loses classical accuracy and won't match published panchangs.
  - E) Other — tell me.

  Which?

● Dwipushkar/Tripushkar research completed — surfacing findings now (Hora question still pending, mentioned at end).

  ⚠️ Material correction — my earlier nakshatra sets were WRONG

  The research found I conflated two unrelated nakshatra groupings. Honest acknowledgment:

  What I claimed (INCORRECT)

  - Dwipushkar: Bharani, Krittika, U.Phalguni, U.Ashadha, U.Bhadrapada (ubhayadaivata / dual-deity nakshatras)
  - Tripushkar: Punarvasu, U.Phalguni, Vishakha, U.Ashadha, Krittika (5 nakshatras)

  What research verified as the CORRECT classical rule

  The rule is structural — based on how each nakshatra's 4 padas (3°20' each) split across zodiacal signs:

  - Dvipada (2-2 pada split) → Dwipushkar nakshatras:
  | Nakshatra  | Pada split               |
  |------------|--------------------------|
  | Mrigashira | Taurus 2 | Gemini 2      |
  | Chitra     | Virgo 2 | Libra 2        |
  | Dhanishta  | Capricorn 2 | Aquarius 2 |

  - 3 nakshatras total, not 5. My earlier set had zero overlap with the correct set.
  - Tripada (3-1 or 1-3 pada split) → Tripushkar nakshatras:
  | Nakshatra        | Pada split                  |
  |------------------|-----------------------------|
  | Krittika         | Aries 1 | Taurus 3          |
  | Punarvasu        | Gemini 3 | Cancer 1         |
  | Uttara Phalguni  | Leo 1 | Virgo 3             |
  | Vishakha         | Libra 3 | Scorpio 1         |
  | Uttara Ashadha   | Sagittarius 1 | Capricorn 3 |
  | Purva Bhadrapada | Aquarius 3 | Pisces 1       |

  - 6 nakshatras total, not 5. I was missing Purva Bhadrapada.

  What stays verified from my earlier claim

  - Tithi set: 2, 7, 12 (Bhadra tithis) ✅
  - Vaara set: Sunday, Tuesday, Saturday (malefic-lord days — Sun, Mars, Saturn) ✅

  Why this matters computationally

  - The two yogas have no overlap (disjoint nakshatra sets by construction). "Overlap resolution rule" from my earlier explanation is a non-problem — dissolves once 
  correct sets are used.
  - My earlier example where I said "today is Dwipushkar active (Shukla Dwitiya + Sunday + Krittika)" — that was WRONG. Krittika is Tripada, not Dvipada, so today   
  would be Tripushkar, not Dwipushkar.
  - Since the rule is arithmetic (pada-count, not convention), no classical source meaningfully disagrees. Single canonical table.
  - Kalaprakashika (Kerala) was not directly verified in this research session, but since the rule is structural not nomenclatural, divergence is not expected.      
  Flagged for physical-copy verification if Josi has meaningful Malayalam user base.

  Corrected canonical rule for Josi

  Dwipushkar = tithi ∈ {2, 7, 12}
             ∧ vaara ∈ {Sunday, Tuesday, Saturday}
             ∧ nakshatra ∈ {Mrigashira, Chitra, Dhanishta}

  Tripushkar = tithi ∈ {2, 7, 12}
             ∧ vaara ∈ {Sunday, Tuesday, Saturday}
             ∧ nakshatra ∈ {Krittika, Punarvasu, U.Phalguni, Vishakha,
                            U.Ashadha, P.Bhadrapada}

  Disjoint by construction. No overlap logic needed.

  Q — Dwipushkar/Tripushkar final decision (post-research)

  - A) Adopt corrected canonical rule as shown above. Single standardized table, no source-selector. Same for both user types. Rationale: rule is arithmetic
  (pada-structure), not convention-based; no classical source meaningfully disagrees.
  - B) Adopt corrected canonical rule + expose a "pada-structure explanation" diagnostic for astrologer (shows why each nakshatra is in which group based on
  sign-boundary pada split).
  - C) Adopt corrected canonical rule + flag Kalaprakashika verification as a future-work item for Malayalam user-base scaling.
  - D) Other.

  classical_names:

    sa_iast: "Yoginī Daśā"         # Sanskrit romanized
    sa_devanagari: "योगिनी दशा"   # Sanskrit script
    ta: "யோகினி தசை"              # Tamil

  So Tamil names are already supported as one of 4 script/language fields. The question is: what's the display default?

  Q — Tamil naming display policy (both user types):

  - A) Tamil display for EVERYONE — nakshatras, planets, zodiac signs always rendered in Tamil script for both B2C and Astrologer, regardless of user region.        
  Sanskrit/English shown only as secondary/tooltip. (Strong interpretation of your literal words.)
  - B) Tamil display for Tamil-region users — B2C auto-adapts by user's region/language: Tamil users see Tamil, Hindi-speakers see Devanagari, English/diaspora see  
  English+Sanskrit-IAST, etc. Astrologer chooses script via profile setting (defaults to their region). (Aligns with the regional-auto-adapt pattern we already      
  locked for panchangam.)
  - C) Multilingual always — show all 4 scripts side-by-side for every entity (e.g., "Krittika / கார்த்திகை / कृत्तिका / Kṛttikā"). Dense but comprehensive. Works fo   or
  astrologer; may be overwhelming for B2C.
  - D) Your personal/testing context only — you want Tamil names for YOUR review sessions (because you're a Tamil-speaking Parashari astrologer), but production     
  display for end-users and other astrologers follows B (regional auto-adapt). This is a reviewer-preference, not a product-wide policy.
  - E) Tamil as primary for Astrologer tier; B2C auto-adapts by region — Tamil is THE script astrologers see by default (because Josi's founder practice is Tamil    
  Parashari); B2C end-users see their own region's script.
  - F) Other — tell me specifically.

  prefer Tamil script would also feel wrong. Which interpretation matches your intent?

  translations in the canonical data tables. Want me to spawn a research agent to produce a comprehensive Tamil-naming audit + fill-in table?

● Tamil naming convention applied. Continuing with next Panchangam nuance using Sanskrit-IAST / Tamil format.

  ---
  Q — Festival calendar source + regional festival scope

  Context. Festival rule engine (item #6 in build priority under phase 2) generates named-day flags: Ekādaśī / ஏகாதசி, Pradoṣa / பிரதோஷம், Saṅkaṣṭī Caturthī / சங்கடஹ ஹர
  சதுர்த்தி, Pūrṇimā / பௌர்ணமி, Amāvāsyā / அமாவாசை, plus named festivals (Dīpāvalī / தீபாவளி, Holī / ஹோலி, Navarātri / நவராத்திரி, Janmāṣṭamī / ஜென்மாஷ்டமி, Rāma Nav      vamī / ராம நவமி, Gaṇeśa
  Caturthī / விநாயக சதுர்த்தி, Śivarātri / சிவராத்திரி, etc.).

  With Siddhānta locked as Dṛk (Drik) default + Vākya (Vakya) astrologer option, and Māsa convention auto-adapting by region, festival dates can still shift across  
  regions for two reasons:

  1. All-India festivals with regional date-calculation rules. Same festival, slightly different date between regions.
    - E.g., Dīpāvalī / தீபாவளி: Amanta tradition (South + West) celebrates on Āśvayuja Kṛṣṇa Caturdaśī (Naraka Caturdaśī day) evening; Purnimanta tradition (North) oon
  Kārttika Kṛṣṇa Amāvāsyā same calendar day but named differently.
    - E.g., Śivarātri / சிவராத்திரி: Māgha Kṛṣṇa Caturdaśī (Amanta — South) vs Phālguna Kṛṣṇa Caturdaśī (Purnimanta — North) — same calendar day, different māsa-namee.
  Masa auto-adapt (3.6) already handles this.
  2. Region-exclusive festivals — festivals celebrated in only one region.
    - Tamil: Poṅgal / பொங்கல் (Makara Saṅkrānti morning — Tamil Nadu), Tamiḻ Puttāṇḍu / தமிழ் புத்தாண்டு (Chithirai 1), Ādi Perukku / ஆடி பெருக்கு, Kārttikai Dīpam க     கார்த்திகை தீபம்,
  Vināyaka Catūrthī / விநாயக சதுர்த்தி
    - Malayalam: Vishu / விஷு (Meṣa Saṅkrānti morning), Onam / ஓணம் (Chingam Śravaṇa nakshatra)
    - Punjabi: Baisākhī (Meṣa Saṅkrānti morning)
    - Bengali: Poilā Boiśākh (solar Baiśākh 1), Durgā Pūjā (Navarātri emphasis)
    - Gujarati: Bestu Varṣ (Vikrama Saṁvat start, Kārttika Śukla 1), Labh Pañcamī
    - Marathi: Gudi Pāḍwā (Caitra Śukla 1)

  Q — Festival calendar policy:

  - A) Single unified festival calendar using Dṛk Siddhānta + māsa auto-adapt. All-India festivals render on one date; māsa-name differs by region (Amanta/Purnimanta
   auto-adapt already locked). Region-exclusive festivals NOT included. Simpler but loses regional identity.
  - B) Unified core + regional additive layers. One all-India festival calendar (~40 named festivals: Dīpāvalī, Holī, Navarātri, Janmāṣṭamī, Rāma Navamī, Gaṇeśa     
  Caturthī, Śivarātri, Akṣaya Tṛtīyā, Guru Pūrṇimā, Rakṣā Bandhan, Dhana Trayodaśī, Bhāi Dooj, Vasanta Pañcamī, etc.) + regional-additive festival tags (Tamil       
  additions, Malayalam additions, Bengali additions, etc.) that surface only for users in that region. B2C sees core + their region's additions. Astrologer sees     
  everything + region toggle. ← my recommendation
  - C) Fully regional — maintain 6-8 separate festival calendars (Tamil, Malayalam, Telugu, Kannada, North-Hindi, Bengali, Gujarati, Marathi), no unified core.      
  User's region drives which calendar applies. Most authentic to how Indian festivals actually work; most expensive to maintain.
  - D) Defer — create a separate festival PRD (or fold into E10 Prasna/Horary or E6a Transit) with its own scope review.
  - E) Research — spawn agent to enumerate exact regional festival lists + verify date-calculation rules for cross-regional festivals before locking.

  Which?

  Examples I now understand:
  - Uttara Āṣāḍhā → Uthiradam (உத்திராடம்)
  - Daśā → Dasai (தசை)
  - Horā → Horai (ஹோரை)

  Extending the pattern:
  - Nakshatra → Natchathiram (நட்சத்திரம்)
  - Rasi → Rasi (ராசி)
  - Muhūrta → Muhurtham (முஹூர்த்தம்)
  - Ghati → Nazhigai (நாழிகை) — Tamil-specific term
  - Yamagaṇḍa → Emakandam (எமகண்டம்) — Tamil-specific
  - Gulika Kāla → Kuligai (குளிகை) — Tamil-specific
  - Pradoṣa → Pradosham (பிரதோஷம்)
  - Ekādaśī → Ekadasi (ஏகாதசி)
  - Pūrṇimā → Pournami (பௌர்ணமி)
  - Amāvāsyā → Amavasai (அமாவாசை)
  - Poṅgal → Pongal (பொங்கல்)
  - Saṅkrānti → Sankaranthi (சங்கராந்தி)
  - Pushya → Poosam (பூசம்)
  - Mṛgaśīrā → Mirugasirsham (மிருகசீர்ஷம்)
  - Kṛttikā → Karthikai (கார்த்திகை)
  - Sūrya → Sooriyan (சூரியன்)
  - Bṛhaspati/Guru → Guru (குரு) or Viyaazhan (வியாழன்)

  Q — Festival calendar source + regional festival scope

  Festival rule engine generates named-day flags:
  - Core tithi-recurring: Ekadasi (ஏகாதசி), Pradosham (பிரதோஷம்), Sankatahara Chathurthi (சங்கடஹர சதுர்த்தி), Pournami (பௌர்ணமி), Amavasai (அமாவாசை)
  - Named festivals: Deepavali (தீபாவளி), Navarathri (நவராத்திரி), Krishna Jayanthi (கிருஷ்ண ஜெயந்தி), Rama Navami (ராம நவமி), Vinayaka Chathurthi (விநாயக சதுர்த்தி)     ), Shivarathri
  (சிவராத்திரி), Akshaya Thrithiyai (அட்சய திருதியை), Guru Pournami (குரு பௌர்ணமி), etc.

  With Siddhantha locked (Drik default + Vakya astrologer option) and Masam auto-adapting by region, cross-regional divergence still arises from two sources:        

  Source 1 — Same festival, regional date-calculation differences.
  - Deepavali (தீபாவளி): Amanta (South/West) celebrates Ashwayuja Krishna Chathurdasi evening; Purnimanta (North) calls it Karthika Krishna Amavasai. Same calendar dday
  — Masam auto-adapt (3.6) handles the name difference.
  - Shivarathri (சிவராத்திரி): Magha Krishna Chathurdasi (Amanta) = Phalguna Krishna Chathurdasi (Purnimanta). Same day, handled by Masam auto-adapt.

  Source 2 — Region-exclusive festivals (not celebrated nationwide):
  - Tamil: Pongal (பொங்கல்) — Makara Sankaranthi morning · Tamil Puthandu (தமிழ் புத்தாண்டு) — Chithirai 1 · Aadi Perukku (ஆடி பெருக்கு) · Karthigai Deepam (கார்த்தி       ிகை தீபம்)
  - Malayalam: Vishu (விஷு) — Mesha Sankaranthi · Onam (ஓணம்) — Chingam Thiruvonam
  - Telugu/Kannada: Ugadi (உகாதி) — Chithirai Shukla Prathami
  - Punjabi: Baisakhi — Mesha Sankaranthi
  - Bengali: Poila Boishakh — solar Boishakh 1 · Durga Puja emphasis
  - Gujarati: Bestu Varsh — Karthika Shukla 1 · Labh Panchami
  - Marathi: Gudi Padwa — Chithirai Shukla 1

  Q — Festival calendar policy:

  - A) Single unified calendar using Drik Siddhantha + Masam auto-adapt. Region-exclusive festivals NOT included. Loses regional identity.
  - B) Unified core + regional additive layers — ~40 all-India festivals as core + region-specific additions (Tamil, Malayalam, Telugu, Kannada, Punjabi, Bengali,   
  Gujarati, Marathi) that surface only for users in that region. B2C sees core + their region's additions. Astrologer sees everything with region-toggle. ← my       
  recommendation
  - C) Fully regional — 6-8 separate calendars, no shared core. Most authentic; highest maintenance.
  - D) Defer — create a dedicated festival PRD under P2.
  - E) Research — spawn agent to enumerate exact regional festival lists + verify date-calculation rules before locking.

  Which?

                                                                                                                                                                     ❯ what  is Ashtottari eligibility predicate                                                                                                                                                            
● Ashtottari Dasai eligibility predicate                                                                                                                             
                                                                                                                                                                     
  Background. Ashtottari Dasai is a 108-year dasai system (8 Mahadasais: Sooriyan 6yr + Chandran 15yr + Sevvai 8yr + Budhan 17yr + Sani 10yr + Guru 19yr + Rahu 12yr   + Sukkiran 21yr = 108yr). It's one of the three dasai systems E1a PRD adds on top of Vimshottari (Vimshottari + Yogini + Ashtottari).                              
                                                                                                                                                                       Unlike Vimshottari and Yogini — which always apply to every chart — Ashtottari has a classical eligibility condition. The chart must pass the condition to           "qualify" for Ashtottari; if it doesn't qualify, classical tradition says Ashtottari shouldn't be used for that chart.                                                                                                                                                                                                                    Two classical schools disagree on the condition:                                                                                                                   

  Strict school (BPHS Ch.47 v.2 — literal reading)

  Ashtottari applies ONLY when ALL of these hold:
  1. Chandran is in Krishna Paksham (waning half — tithi 16-30, i.e., day after Pournami to day before Amavasai)
  2. Lagnam is occupied by OR aspected by a natural benefic (Guru, Sukkiran, or well-placed Budhan)
  3. Night birth (sunset to sunrise)
  4. Some commentators add: Rahu not in Lagnam

  If all 4 conditions met → Ashtottari is the "applicable" dasai for that chart (used alongside Vimshottari as cross-reference). Otherwise → skip Ashtottari.        

  Practical consequence: ~10-15% of charts meet all 4 conditions. Most Strict-school astrologers simply don't compute Ashtottari for charts that don't qualify.      

  Permissive school (Jataka Parijata + most modern practice)

  Ashtottari applies universally to every chart. The BPHS conditions are treated as a lineage preference or a "strength indicator" (when conditions met, Ashtottari  
  is dominant; otherwise just cross-reference), not a hard gate.

  Practical consequence: ALL charts get Ashtottari computed and shown. Modern panchangams (Jagannatha Hora, Parashara's Light, JyotishStar) follow this approach.    

  Simplified strict variant

  Some schools use only condition #1 (Krishna Paksham) and ignore the Lagnam-benefic and night-birth clauses. Middle ground between strict and permissive.

  ---
  Current Josi E1a PRD proposes: Permissive default

  Rationale (per PRD):
  - Aligns with Jagannatha Hora 7.x behavior — differential testing works
  - B2C users expect to see their dasai without lineage-specific gating
  - Astrologer pro-mode can filter to strict via per-astrologer preference (via astrologer_source_preference table from F2)

  ---
  Q — Ashtottari Dasai eligibility for both user types

  - A) Permissive default (PRD current) for both user types. Ashtottari always computed and shown. Astrologer can optionally filter via their profile preference.    
  - B) Strict BPHS Ch.47 v.2 for both user types — all 4 conditions (Krishna Paksham + Lagnam-benefic + night birth + Rahu-not-in-Lagnam) must hold. Ashtottari      
  hidden/not-computed if not qualified.
  - C) Simplified strict (Krishna Paksham only) for both user types. Only the paksham condition; ignore the other 3.
  - D) Permissive for B2C + Strict for Astrologer — end-user always sees Ashtottari. Astrologer sees Ashtottari only when BPHS conditions met; a
  "qualified/not-qualified" flag appears on the chart. Astrologer can override per chart.
  - E) Permissive default for both, but emit a classical-strictness flag on the Ashtottari output (applicable: strict_qualified | simplified_strict_qualified |      
  permissive_only) — UI decides what to do with the flag. Astrologer sees the flag always.
  - F) Research — check how Kerala Vakya panchangams and Tamil muhurta-focused practice handle this.

  Which?
